result: Moonlit avenues have their own hum, a low, patient drone that pretends to be silence while actually ferrying a thousand tiny stories. I noticed this last night as I walked home from a late train, collar turned up, pockets stuffed with yesterday’s ticket stubs and the receipt for a watermelon I never bought. The sidewalk glistened from a brief shower, and every sodium lamp painted a golden ellipse, like borrowed halos for anyone willing to stand still. In the distance, a saxophone rehearsed the same mournful bar over and over, polishing melancholy until it shone like chrome. Passing a shuttered bakery, I smelled phantom cinnamon and imagined the baker dreaming of deserts, palms rustling over dunes made of sifted sugar. A stray cat escorted me for half a block, keeping aristocratic distance, pausing only to glare at a flickering neon sign that promised fortune telling upstairs. I climbed the steps, found the door locked, yet the sign continued insisting Someone Is In. Perhaps the future was busy elsewhere. When I reached my building, the elevator yawned and then declined to cooperate, so I took the stairs, counting them like rosary beads, promising to start stretching tomorrow. At the landing between floors three and four, a window was cracked open, letting in the faint peppery scent of rain-soaked concrete. I leaned out, watched the city re-button its coat, and thought about how every night writes its own liner notes, but few people bother to flip the record and read them. Tonight.
